chunk_summary: Stepwise management of constipation in former preterm infants emphasizes hydration, correct feeding practices, non-pharmacologic interventions, and safe use of osmotic laxatives like lactulose or PEG, while screening for red flags and monitoring growth and stooling patterns.
learning_objectives: Understand the common causes of constipation in former preterm infants post-discharge.
Learn non-pharmacologic strategies for managing constipation, including hydration and feeding adjustments.
Recognize safe and effective use of osmotic laxatives like lactulose and PEG in preterm infants.
Identify red flags requiring further evaluation in infants with constipation.
key_facts: Constipation is often caused by incorrect formula mixing (e.g., too little water) or low fluid intake.
Lactulose dosage: 1–3 mL/kg/day divided once or twice daily, titrated to soft stools.
PEG dosage: 0.4–1 g/kg/day for maintenance, adjusted to response and reassessed frequently.
Non-pharmacologic measures include tummy time, abdominal massage, and avoiding early solids or unnecessary formula thickening.
Red flags include bilious vomiting, severe abdominal distension, failure to pass meconium, blood in stool without fissure, fever, or weight loss with dehydration.
reference_quote: Evaluation and treatment of functional constipation in infants and children: evidence-based recommendations (NASPGHAN/ESPGHAN) — https://www.naspghan.org.
